content: Vaccines function by presenting antigens derived from pathogens, either as attenuated organisms, inactivated particles, or recombinant proteins, to prime the adaptive immune system without causing disease. Upon administration, antigen-presenting cells internalize these vaccine components, processing and presenting epitopes via MHC class I and II molecules to naïve CD8+ and CD4+ T cells, respectively. This cognate interaction, along with co-stimulatory signals, drives clonal expansion and differentiation of effector T cells, including cytotoxic T lymphocytes and T follicular helper cells, which are critical for orchestrating adaptive immunity. Concurrently, B cells recognizing the antigen undergo activation, often in germinal centers facilitated by T follicular helper cells, leading to affinity maturation and isotype switching that optimize antibody specificity and effector functions. The generation of long-lived plasma cells and memory B and T lymphocytes ensures rapid and robust responses upon subsequent pathogen exposure, a hallmark of durable vaccine-induced immunity. Importantly, many vaccines incorporate adjuvants that engage pattern recognition receptors on innate immune cells, amplifying cytokine production and enhancing antigen presentation to potentiate adaptive responses. However, antigenic drift and immune escape mechanisms necessitate continual vaccine updates or design strategies that elicit broadly neutralizing antibodies and cross-reactive T cell responses. Advances in mRNA and vector-based platforms now enable precise antigen design and delivery, accelerating the induction of potent, durable immunity tailored to evolving pathogens.
